statement.p: An object was shot up into the air with an initial vertical speed of 576 feet per second. Its height as time passes can be modeled by the quadratic equation y={-16t^{2}+576t}. Here t represents the number of seconds since the objects release, and y represents the objects height in feet.

li.p: After , this object reached its maximum height of .
This object flew for before it landed on the ground.
This object was in the air {12\ {\rm s}} after its release.
This object was {4400\ {\rm ft}} high at two times: once after its release, and again later after its release.

solution.p: The function f given by the rule f(t)={-16t^{2}+576t} is quadratic. Identify that a=-16, b=576, c=0.

li.p: Since a\lt 0, this parabola opens down. Its vertex represents the maximum height the object can go and when it got there.

solution.p: To find the vertex, we first find the parabolas axis of symmetry by the axis formula:
Next, we substitute t= 18 into fs formula, and we have:
The parabolas vertex is at {\left(18,5184\right)}. This implies that {18\ {\rm s}} after the release, the object reached its maximum height of {5184\ {\rm ft}}.

li.p: The parabolas x-intercepts represent the moments when the object was at a height of 0: on the ground. We will solve the equation f(t)=0 to find its x-intercepts. We have:

solution.p: This parabola has two x-intercepts: (0,0) and (36,0). The point (0,0) represents the moment when the object was released; the point (36,0) implies the object fell back to the ground after {36\ {\rm s}}.

li.p: To find the objects height 12 seconds after it was released, we substitute t=12 into the fomrula for f, and we have:

solution.p: This result shows that {12\ {\rm s}} after the object was released, it was {4608\ {\rm ft}} high in the air.

li.p: To find when the object was {4400\ {\rm ft}} high, we solve the equation f(t)={4400}.

solution.p: For such big numbers, its easier to use the Quadratic Formula to solve this equation. Identify that a=-16,b=576,c={-4400}. We have:

p.m: \begin{aligned} t \amp = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2}-4ac}}{2a} \\ t \amp = \frac{-(576) \pm \sqrt{(576)^{2}-4(-16)({-4400})}}{2(-16)} \\ t \amp = \frac{-576 \pm \sqrt{{50176}}}{-32} \\ t \amp = \frac{-576 \pm {224}}{-32} \\ t \amp = \frac{-576 + {224}}{-32} \amp \text{or} \amp \amp t=\frac{-576 - {224}}{-32} \\ t \amp = 11 \amp \text{or} \amp \amp t=25 \end{aligned}

solution.p: This result shows that the object was {4400\ {\rm ft}} high twice during its flight: once {11\ {\rm s}} after it was released (on its way up), and another time 25 seconds after it was released (on its way down).
Its important to understand the role of each part of this question in the graph of f.
